title: Financial Independence Early Retirement (FIRE) Strategy - Client 011
content: Achieving financial independence and optional early retirement requires disciplined savings, aggressive investing, and meticulous planning to accumulate sufficient assets supporting perpetual withdrawals. FIRE Framework: Client-011, age 38, current income $285K, expenses $95K annually, targeting retirement at age 48. Required portfolio: $2.375M using 4% safe withdrawal rate ($95K annual spending). Current portfolio: $1.1M. Gap: $1.275M over 10 years. Savings Strategy: Maximize tax-advantaged contributions: $23K to 401(k) with $10K employer match, $7K to Roth IRA for both spouses ($14K total), $4.3K to HSA. Taxable account savings of $110K annually. Total annual savings: $161.3K (57% savings rate). Employer contributions and investment returns accelerate timeline. Investment Allocation: Aggressive growth portfolio given 10-year accumulation phase and 40+ year spending horizon: 85% stocks (60% US total market, 25% international), 10% REITs and alternatives, 5% bonds for rebalancing opportunities. Expected 8% annual returns with disciplined contributions achieve target. Withdrawal Strategy: Initial years bridge to age 59.5 when retirement accounts accessible: Withdrawal from taxable accounts using tax-efficient LTCG rates, Roth IRA contributions (not earnings) accessible penalty-free, 72(t) SEPP withdrawals if needed for IRA access. After age 59.5, traditional retirement withdrawal sequencing. Healthcare Planning: Critical FIRE component - ACA marketplace coverage until Medicare eligibility. HSA contributions and accumulation creating tax-free healthcare fund. Strategic income management optimizing premium subsidies. Geographic Arbitrage: Consider lower cost-of-living areas post-retirement reducing required portfolio size. Potential reduction to $80K annual expenses saves $375K required assets accelerating timeline 2 years. Contingency Planning: Part-time work or consulting during initial retirement years reducing portfolio stress. Flexibility to delay retirement if market conditions unfavorable (sequence of returns risk).
